authoritative_sources.title: Required Equipment for Barbering and Cosmetology Establishments
authoritative_sources.publisher: Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TDLR is an official cosmetology regulator, and this establishment guidance defines required facility, ventilation, inspection-report, and current-laws-book conditions that materially change salon readiness.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Facility and establishment readiness before a service can safely advance

authoritative_sources.title: Inspections Guide
authoritative_sources.publisher: Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TDLR's inspection guide is an official operating reference for salon inspections and shows what records, licenses, sanitation practices, and client-safety conditions matter in day-to-day salon operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Workstation readiness, client protection, sanitation checks, and inspection-facing execution

authoritative_sources.title: Penalties and Sanctions for Practitioners and Establishments
authoritative_sources.publisher: Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TDLR's sanction schedule is authoritative for which salon control failures drive enforcement, including missing inspection-visible records, prohibited service conditions, sanitation failures, and off-site scheduling violations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Failure handling, closeout evidence, and enforcement-sensitive exception routing

authoritative_sources.title: Health Hazards in Nail Salons
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA is the federal worker-safety authority, and its salon-specific hazard page defines the recognized chemical, biological, ergonomic, and worker-rights risk posture for nail-salon work that often coexists inside broader salon operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Hazard recognition and worker-safety baseline during routine salon execution

authoritative_sources.title: Health Hazards in Nail Salons - Standards
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA's standards page directly links salon work to enforceable bloodborne-pathogen, hazard-communication, PPE, respiratory-protection, and related safety controls that limit safe operational action.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Chemical, biological, and PPE control verification before or during service

authoritative_sources.title: Hair Salons - Formaldehyde in Your Products
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA's hair-salon product guidance is an official reference for when product labels, SDS access, ventilation, air-testing escalation, and training become mandatory control questions in salon operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Chemical-service evidence review and product-safety escalation
used_for: product label and SDS dependency handling

authoritative_sources.title: Stay Healthy and Safe While Giving Manicures and Pedicures
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA's salon worker guide is a public, lane-specific workflow reference for routine manicure and pedicure execution, ventilation, work practices, disinfecting, and exposure reduction in live service settings.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Routine service setup, work practices, turnover, and exposure reduction

authoritative_sources.title: NIC Cosmetology Practical Examination Candidate Information Bulletin
authoritative_sources.publisher: National-Interstate Council of State Boards of Cosmetology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIC is the recognized interstate cosmetology testing body used by state boards, and its practical bulletin supplies the public setup, client protection, chemical-service, cleanup, and blood-exposure vocabulary that salon operators encounter in actual practice.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Client preparation, practical service execution, cleanup, and blood-exposure handling

workflow_stages.name: Appointment and establishment readiness
workflow_stages.description: Confirm that the service is tied to a real appointment, location, practitioner assignment, and establishment setup with the required facility, inspection-facing, and jurisdiction context before execution proceeds.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Pre-service client protection and workstation setup
workflow_stages.description: Validate consultation, contraindication flags, service history, patch-test or formula artifacts when relevant, and that tools, stations, or foot spas are cleaned and ready before work starts.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Active service execution and hazard control
workflow_stages.description: Advance routine salon work only when chemical, biological, ventilation, PPE, and blood-exposure controls are understood and the service stays inside documented practitioner and establishment boundaries.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Post-service turnover and completion evidence capture
workflow_stages.description: Require a source-of-truth service ticket, checkout state, turnover or sanitation evidence, and any incident or exception logging before closing the work.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Safety, policy, and authority exception routing
workflow_stages.description: Stop routine progression and route to licensed, compliance, facility, or billing owners when client safety, licensing scope, chemical controls, or policy-exception questions move outside the lane.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: appointment record, service menu item, and provider assignment
client consultation form, service history, patch-test note, and formula card when applicable
service ticket, check-in or checkout timestamps, and lifecycle state history
practitioner license, establishment evidence, inspection-facing report, and current laws-book presence
tool disinfection log, workstation turnover record, foot spa cleaning log, and disinfectant container labeling
product labels, safety data sheets, ventilation notes, and chemical-service instructions
incident note, blood-exposure response record, and blocked-work or escalation note
completion or fulfillment record with customer-impact and next-action status
systems_of_record: client profile, consultation, and service-history repository
staff scheduling and practitioner assignment roster
incident, exception, and escalation tracking system
tenant policy repository for jurisdiction, refund, minors, and mobile-service rules

allowed: assess whether visible salon evidence supports progressing, blocking, reworking, or completing routine service execution
organize missing prerequisites, next owners, and residual customer-impact risks
summarize appointment, service, sanitation, product-safety, and incident conflicts without claiming final compliance or licensed authority
escalate: client conditions, injuries, infections, or blood-exposure facts that require licensed provider, compliance, or manager ownership
chemical-product uncertainty, missing SDS or label evidence, or ventilation questions that materially change service safety
service requests outside practitioner or establishment authority, including off-site or mobile conditions that need jurisdiction-specific review
refunds, discounts, redo authority, or customer-compensation requests outside the lane
conflicting public guidance and tenant policy on sanitation, service eligibility, or closure criteria
refuse: mark work complete without appointment, service-ticket, and turnover or incident evidence
treat salon work as generic front-desk activity without named salon records and systems
override sanitation, product-safety, licensing, or client-protection requirements
diagnose, authorize, or waive client contraindications, infections, or other safety-critical judgments

domain_failure_modes: closing a service from a generic CRM or note instead of the salon booking and service-ticket systems
progressing a service when chair, workstation, tool, or foot spa readiness is not evidenced
treating open wounds, infection indicators, or blood-exposure events as routine customer-service issues
running chemical services without product identity, SDS access, label review, or ventilation context
ignoring off-site, mobile, or license-scope boundaries because the request sounds like ordinary salon work
allowing refund, redo, or policy-override pressure to drive unsupported completion
flattening salon-specific terminology and artifacts into generic customer-support workflow

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: State-by-state differences in salon establishment, mobile-service, and service-scope rules
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: The exact prerequisites for off-site services, mobile work, foot spa cleaning, minors handling, and practitioner scope vary materially by jurisdiction.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require runtime retrieval of the governing jurisdiction and active rule overlay before final progression when those facts could change the result.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific consultation, patch-test, formula-card, and closeout requirements
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Public sources define the need for evidence-backed client protection and closure, but tenants vary in exactly which service artifacts are mandatory for each service type.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require tenant retrieval for service-type-specific artifact mapping and preserve the public safety baseline when local forms differ.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Operating-model differences across employee salons, booth-rental suites, and blended multi-location chains
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Assignment ownership, facility responsibilities, and inspection-facing record custody can shift by operating model.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require tenant operating-model retrieval and keep establishment, sanitation, and customer-impact checks explicit rather than inferred.

notes: The public baseline anchors salon readiness, sanitation, client-safety, chemical-safety, and completion evidence, but tenant retrieval is still required for service menus, local rules, refund authority, and operating-model details.
The specialist is intentionally structured as salon operations labor and must not imply licensed provider, compliance, legal, or billing approval authority.
